content.full_text:
California
((i9)
Fair Political
Practices Commission
December 7,
1993
Nancy Leneis, Legislative Aide
Office of Assemblymember
Richard Mountjoy
Asselublymember,
59th District
State Capitol
P.O.
Box 942848
Sacramento,
CA
94248-0001
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No.
A-93—459
Dear Ms. Leneis:
This is in response to your request for confirmation of
telephone advice provided to you on December 1,
1993, regarding
the gift provisions of the Political Reform Act
(the “Act”).
1
This letter confirms that your letter dated December2,
1993,
accurately summarizes the advice I provided to you on that date.
You stated that you were offered tickets to the 12th Annual
Celebrity Luncheon which is being held as a fundraising event for
the American Lung Association by Western-Pacific Roofing
Corporation.
You asked whether you could accept these tickets.
I advised you that the Act requires public officials to
disclose economic interests,
including donors of gifts, that could
foreseeably be affected by the exercise of the official’s duties.
(Section 87207.)
The Act further prohibits any public official
from participating in or influencing any decision that will
foreseeably and materially affect a donor of,
or any intermediary
or agent for a donor of,
a gift or gifts aggregating $250 or more
in value provided to, received by,
or promised to the public
official within
12 months prior to the time when the decision is
made.
(Section 87100; Section 87103(e).)
And finally, the Act now
provides restrictions on the receipt of gifts and honoraria.
1
Government Code Sections 81000—91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations,
Sections
18000—18954.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95804-0807 •
(916) 322-5660


Our File No.
A-93—459
Page
2
Section 89505(a)
and Regulation 18954 provide that no designated
employee of a state agency may accept gifts in excess of $270 from
any single source in any calendar year.
However,
as we discussed, Regulation 18946.4(b)
provides:
A gift of a ticket or other admission
privilege to a specific fundraising event shall be
valued as set forth below.
(b)
Where the event is a fundraising event
for an organization exempt from taxation under
Section 501(c) (3)
of the Internal Revenue Code, the
ticket or other admission privilege has no value.
Since the American Lung Association is an organization exempt
from taxation under Section 501(c) (3)
of the Internal Revenue
Code, pursuant to Regulation 18946.4(b), the tickets to the event
have no value and would create no obligations with respect to
these provisions of the Act.
2
(g, Nalera Advice Letter,
No.
A-
92—557.)
If you have any further questions regarding thiè matter,
please feel free to contact me at
(916)
322-5901.
Sincerely,
Steven G.
Churchwell
neral Counsel
By:
John W. Wallace
Counsel, Legal Division
2
Please note,
however, that this conclusion applies to the cost
of the tickets.
If you receive additional free benefits that are
not a part of the ticket price, these free benefits would be
considered gifts for purposes of the Act.
Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries.
Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of l0 per page.

sections.question: Whether a legislative aide could accept tickets to a fundraising event (12th Annual Celebrity Luncheon for the American Lung Association) offered by Western-Pacific Roofing Corporation.
sections.conclusion: Since the American Lung Association is an organization exempt from taxation under Section 501(c)(3) of the Internal Revenue Code, pursuant to Regulation 18946.4(b), the tickets to the event have no value and would create no obligations with respect to the gift provisions of the Act. However, any additional free benefits received that are not part of the ticket price would be considered gifts for purposes of the Act.
sections.question_synthetic: Can a designated state employee accept complimentary tickets to a charitable fundraising event offered by a potential donor under the Political Reform Act's gift restrictions?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The tickets have no reportable value under Regulation 18946.4(b) because they are for a Section 501(c)(3) charitable fundraising event, so they do not trigger gift restrictions or disclosure requirements. Additional benefits beyond the ticket itself would, however, be subject to gift limitations.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document confirms telephone advice from December 1, 1993. Clear question and conclusion sections present. Footnote 2 provides important caveat regarding additional free benefits.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether a legislative aide could accept tickets to a fundraising event (12th Annual Celebrity Luncheon for the American Lung Association) offered by Western-Pacific Roofing Corporation.

CONCLUSION: Since the American Lung Association is an organization exempt from taxation under Section 501(c)(3) of the Internal Revenue Code, pursuant to Regulation 18946.4(b), the tickets to the event have no value and would create no obligations with respect to the gift provisions of the Act. However, any additional free benefits received that are not part of the ticket price would be considered gifts for purposes of the Act.

embedding.summary: FPPC advises that a state legislative aide may accept complimentary tickets to a charitable fundraising event from a potential donor without violating gift restrictions, as tickets to 501(c)(3) fundraising events have no assigned value under FPPC regulations.
